title: Spin polarisation signatures of Fractionally Charged Skyrmions in Fractional Quantum Hall states
authors: Odysseas Williams, Stefan Faelt, Christian Reichl, Werner Wegscheider
abstract: We investigate spin polarisation and low-energy excitations in fractional quantum Hall (FQH) states using cavity-polariton spectroscopy of high-mobility GaAs quantum wells. By measuring the optical coupling strength of interband Landau-level excitations over the range $1/3 \le ν\le 1$, we extract the spin polarisation of the electron system as a function of filling factor.   Complete suppression of the oscillator strength of the lowest energy excitation, characteristic of singlet trion formation in fully polarised systems, is reported for the first time in this regime.   At large magnetic fields, fully polarised FQH states exhibit symmetric depolarisation away from their quantised fillings, analogous to Skyrmionic behaviour near $ν=1$. The depolarisation follows an empirical law $S=ν^*$, where $S$ is the number of spin flips per added magnetic flux quantum and $ν^*$ the effective Composite Fermion filling factor. We interpret this behaviour as evidence for Minimal Fractionally Charged Skyrmions (MFCS) formed from bound spin-flip and quasiparticle excitations.
summary: This work uses cavity-polariton spectroscopy on GaAs quantum wells to measure spin polarization in Fractional Quantum Hall states. By analyzing the optical response, the authors report that the depolarization away from quantized fillings strongly suggests the presence of Minimal Fractionally Charged Skyrmions (MFCS). This provides experimental evidence for collective spin dynamics in highly correlated electron systems.
main_problem: The study investigates spin polarization and low-energy excitations within Fractional Quantum Hall (FQH) states.
main_result:
The research reports evidence for Minimal Fractionally Charged Skyrmions (MFCS), characterized by a depolarization following the empirical law $S=
u^*$ away from quantized fillings.
method: Spin polarization is probed using cavity-polariton spectroscopy on GaAs quantum wells, measuring the optical coupling strength of interband Landau-level excitations.
model_or_system:
The system involves electrons in Fractional Quantum Hall states ($
u$ between $1/3$ and $1$), analyzed within the framework of Composite Fermion (CF) theory. The experiment uses GaAs quantum wells coupled to optical microcavities.
key_observables: Spin polarization ($P$), oscillator strength suppression, and the depolarization parameter $S$.
important_parameters:
Fractional filling factor $
u$ ($1/3 \le 
u \le 1$), effective Composite Fermion filling factor ($
u^*$), and magnetic field ($B$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that the depolarization away from quantized fillings is governed by the formation of Minimal Fractionally Charged Skyrmions (MFCS).
figures_summary:
Figures show optical transfer-matrix simulations, lowest polariton energy vs. $B$, and plots of extracted spin polarization ($P$) versus filling factor ($
u$ or $
u^*$), comparing data to theoretical models.
paper_structure:
The paper progresses by first establishing the experimental measurement technique (polariton spectroscopy) to extract spin polarization signatures across various FQH states, then analyzing the depolarization behavior away from integer/fractional fillings, and finally interpreting this smooth variation as evidence for MFCS formation governed by $S=
u^*$.
why_interesting: The connection drawn between optical measurements in solid-state systems (GaAs QWs) and exotic topological excitations like Skyrmions provides a tangible experimental realization of complex many-body physics relevant to correlated electron models.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Optical transfer-matrix simulations used to design devices 2λ and 3λ. Only the layers nearest to the central cavity are shown. Blue, green, and yellow layers correspond to AlAs, Al0.1Ga0.9As and GaAs, respectively. The red curve corresponds to the electric field intensity of a confined resonant photon. Numbers next to each layer indicate layer thicknesses (nm); the single differing value highlights the design change in device 3λ. (b) 1 −R spectra of sample 2λ as a function of perpendicular magnetic field B. New polariton branches and changes in polariton splitting associated with integer and fractional ν are marked by white dashed lines. (c) Same measurement for sample 3λ....

FIG. 2. Lowest polariton energy in sample 2λ as a function of magnetic field B, measured at low (blue) and high (red) optical powers differing by a factor of 1.2×104. Integer filling factors corresponding to maxima and minima are labelled in black.

FIG. 3. Three lowest excitation energies E0, E1, and E2 measured in σ−polarisation for sample 2λ in the range 4 T < B < 8 T and sample 3λ in the range 2 T < B < 6.7 T. The schematic on the right illustrates the proposed interpretation: each excitation originates from a different hole subband (0H, 1H, or 2H) and forms an exciton (cross symbol) whose electron component occupies one of the two spin-split lowest LLs. Filled circles denote background electrons and empty circles holes; the illustrated configuration corresponds to an unpolarised ν = 2/3 state. The lowest-energy excitation forms a negative trion, in which a |↑⟩exciton binds a |↓⟩electron.

FIG. 4. Coupling energies Ω0, Ω1, and Ω2 measured for sam- ples (a) 2λ and (b) 3λ. These values are extracted from the same measurements as Fig. 3, with matching colour schemes between figures. Vertical black lines indicate filling factors ν = 2/3, 3/5, and 4/7 (from left to right).

FIG. 5. Spin polarisation P for samples 2λ (blue) and 3λ (red), extracted from the Ω0 measurements of Fig. 4 using Eq. 7. The accompanying diagrams show the corresponding CF configurations: top panels represent fully polarised states, bottom panels partially polarised states. The expected partial polarisations of the ν = 3/5 and 4/7 states, P = 1/3 and 1/2, are indicated by black dotted lines.
